source_title: When the Numbers Don't Add Up: Accommodating Data Journalism
author: Sue Green

extracts.extract: The rich story potential inherent in the vast quantities of data created and captured daily is an opportunity lost to journalism students if they do not learn how to source this data, to analyse and to storify it. It is clear that data journalism skills are now not only a means for journalists to find stories but also they increase the attractiveness of new journalism graduates to employers
extracts.notes: Frames data journalism as three-step process: source, analyse, storify

extracts.extract: Journalists need to be data-savvy … now it's also going to be about poring over data and equipping yourself with the tools to analyse it and picking out what's interesting
extracts.notes: Tim Berners-Lee quote, 2010. Emphasises journalist's role in selecting what is interesting from data.

extracts.extract: Data journalists have also innovated by institutionalising a whole new range of norms and conventions to both legitimise their practice, and overcome generalised public mistrust of journalism. In this respect, data journalism uses methods reinforced by values such as numeric infallibility, scientific rigour, computational neutrality, crowd sourced collaboration, intra-and extra-newsroom cooperation, and hyperlocal empathy to generate exclusives that are generally perceived as more accurate and transparent
extracts.notes: Borges-Rey 2016. Lists epistemic values data journalists use to legitimise practice.

extracts.extract: Knight (2015, p. 70) cautions over 'the appropriation of the language of investigative data journalism for somewhat less rigorous material' while Flew, Spurgeon, Daniel and Swift (2012, p. 167) argue that data journalism is only as good as its data.
extracts.notes: Warning against appropriating investigative DJ language for weak work

extracts.extract: The strength of computational journalism relies on the integrity, quality and reliability of the data available: a central concern for both journalism and information technology. Statistical anomalies, a lack of sense, misinterpretations, conflicting data standards, incomplete data, skewed results or malignly altered data pose serious problems.
extracts.notes: Taxonomy of data quality threats: anomalies, misinterpretations, conflicting standards, incompleteness, skew, tampering

extracts.extract: However important is teaching students to find and analyse data, equally important must be teaching them to verify and assess the reliability of that data.
extracts.notes: Positions verification as co-equal with finding and analysis

extracts.extract: the teaching of data journalism 'frequently neglects fundamental journalistic topics such as ethical issues, transparency, accountability, and responsiveness'
extracts.notes: Splendore et al. 2015. Warns that technical DJ teaching neglects journalistic ethics.

extracts.extract: a majority of the journalists who worked on the data journalism projects samples did so as researchers and had minimal computer skills, while those with graphic design, statistics and computer skills did most of the complex project creation.
extracts.notes: Tabary et al. 2016 Quebec study. Documents role split: journalists as researchers vs technical staff as builders.

extracts.extract: Journalism programs can integrate alternative teaching methods to help fill the gaps in their own faculty. Examples include cooperative teaching among different university departments, online courses, and independent tutorial packs
extracts.notes: Berret and Phillips 2016. Recommends cross-department cooperation, MOOCs, and tutorial packs.

extracts.extract: The postgraduate project demonstrated the value of collaborative teaching to help instructors tackle new curriculum content, including both data analysis skills and innovative approaches to their delivery
extracts.notes: Treadwell et al. 2016. NZ two-university collaboration on gambling investigation as teaching vehicle.

extracts.extract: active engagement with techniques for the large-scale manipulation of data using computing software to enable new ways to access, organise and present information
extracts.notes: Flew et al. 2012 definition of computational journalism. Frames it as access-organise-present.

extracts.extract: Each separately explained that to describe students as mathematics averse and to think of data journalism as about mathematics was a misconception. Rather, it was more accurate to think of those daunted by it as numbers averse, given that modern software, including sorting and scraping tools, meant very little mathematics was required.
extracts.notes: Reframes DJ as numbers-work mediated by software, not raw mathematics. Mentions sorting and scraping tools.

extracts.extract: basic maths and statistics are just journalism
extracts.notes: Jason Thomas (SBS data journalist) via Walkley Foundation. Normalises quantitative work as core journalism.

extracts.extract: Using an open-source software which the students could download during his classes, he has begun with a module in the third year investigative journalism unit, comprising two lectures, tutorial exercises and a requirement that data analysis be incorporated in the student's final major assignment.
extracts.notes: Describes practical integration: open-source software, lectures, exercises, embedded assessment.

extracts.extract: Journalism schools can collaborate across the university to meet the burgeoning need for instruction in data and computation but should be wary of trying to outsource too much—while understanding how to do math, statistics, or computer programming is an important component, data journalism is much more than that.
extracts.notes: Berret and Phillips 2016 warning. DJ requires journalistic judgment beyond technical skills.

extracts.extract: In cases where a data unit was not in operation, a minimum of one data journalist was appointed—or worked informally—to write data-driven journalism or to collaborate with other specialist correspondents in co-authored pieces. In this respect, data journalism practice is fully ingrained within the news cycles of the majority of the mainstream organisations studied.
extracts.notes: Borges-Rey 2016 UK survey. Documents minimum viable DJ staffing: at least one data journalist per newsroom.

extracts.extract: Other university departments or faculties may have expertise which can be drawn on beyond those administrative silos. Guest lectures and small teaching modules inserted into existing courses may overcome issues of a lack of staff expertise and packed curricula. International collaborations can provide opportunities for student intensives like summer schools. A collaborative team approach between journalism and data analytics educators may assist in overcoming the shortage of skilled data journalism lecturers and sessional teachers.
extracts.notes: Four-point summary of Swinburne lessons learned for DJ curriculum integration.

extracts.extract: In a single lecture towards the end of semester, the students are shown the wide range of data on political issues available to them and encouraged to explore these.
extracts.notes: Describes teaching students to discover available political datasets as entry point.

extracts.extract: students were encouraged to draw on the substantial quantity of comparative data available on the Victorian Government's know your council website. Few, however, appeared confident about tapping into and analysing this rich and readily available information.
extracts.chapter: The Challenge of Incorporating Data Journalism
extracts.notes: Concrete example: Victorian 'know your council' as accessible DJ data source. Notes student reluctance.

extracts.extract: Amid claims of journalistic authority over computational proficiency, at least half of regional data journalists felt limited by their inability to write software code, regarding the skill as a powerful enabling agent in data journalism
extracts.notes: Borges-Rey 2016 UK finding. Coding seen as enabling agent, not optional extra.
